_meta.description: Doneness cue reference for SSA SessionRunner. Maps high-level donenessCue values (color|texture|probeTemp|timer|smell|sound|jiggle) to concrete examples, safety guidance, and UI hints. Used for cooking, preservation, and storehouse/meal planning sessions, and supports reverse generation from free text instructions.

schema.label: string

visual: string

fallbackSignals: string

batchSessionIdeas: string

rows.label: Poultry Safe (No Pink, Clear Juices)

visual: No visible pink near the bone or in the thickest part
Juices run clear, not red
texture: Meat fibers feel firm but still moist when pressed with tongs
smell: Savory roasted poultry aroma; no raw or metallic smell
timeHints: Often reached in final 10–15 minutes of roasting
Bone-in pieces may lag behind boneless by 5–10 minutes

fallbackSignals: Pierce near bone; juices run clear and not bloody
No translucent or jelly-like meat against the bone
Allow a 5–10 minute rest to finish carryover cooking
rows.safetyNotes: USDA food-safety guidance: poultry should reach at least 165°F (74°C) in the thickest part. Always err on the side of safety for chicken and turkey.

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Insert probe into thickest part without touching bone; confirm 165°F+ and clear juices.

batchSessionIdeas: Batch roast chicken quarters for the week and shred once they hit 165°F.
Plan preservation session for cooked chicken (shredded and frozen in meal-size packs).

rows.label: Beef Medium (Warm Pink Center)

visual: Brown sear outside with a slight pink band toward the center
texture: Yielding but springy when pressed with a finger or tongs
smell: Deep roasted beef aroma; no raw smell
sound: Sizzle softens compared to hard sear stage
timeHints: Often ~3–5 minutes per side for 1 inch steak (varies by heat)
Roasts may require 45–90 minutes depending on size

fallbackSignals: Finger test: feels like the base of thumb when making a relaxed fist
Slight resistance when sliced, juices light pink
rows.safetyNotes: Whole cuts of beef and lamb can be safely served at lower temps than poultry if handled properly. Ground meat should still reach at least 160°F inside.

phrases: juices lightly pink but not bloody

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Aim for 140–145°F in center, then rest under foil to allow carryover cooking.

batchSessionIdeas: Slice leftover roast beef for sandwiches once cooled; portion and freeze.
Plan gravy or stew session using medium-cooked beef that will be reheated.

rows.label: Bread Done (Hollow Thump / 200–210°F)

visual: Deep golden crust, especially on top and edges
Sides pull slightly away from the pan
texture: Firm crust that springs back slightly with gentle press
smell: Rich baked bread aroma; no raw dough smell
sound: When tapped on bottom, loaf sounds hollow
timeHints: Typically in final 5–10 minutes of baking window
Check earlier during first runs of new recipes

fallbackSignals: Tap bottom of loaf: hollow sound suggests done
Center feels set, not squishy, when pressed lightly
rows.safetyNotes: Undercooked bread will be gummy and dense in the center. Fully cooked crumb helps with storage and mold prevention.

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Aim for 200–210°F inside loaf; look for hollow sound and deep golden crust.

batchSessionIdeas: Schedule weekly bread-baking batch and cool completely before slicing and freezing.
Tie bread-doneness checks into grain usage planning (how many loaves per bucket).

rows.label: Golden Brown Edges, Light Center
examples: Drop cookies with crisp edges and chewy centers

visual: Edges have clear golden-brown color
Centers slightly lighter but opaque
texture: Edges feel firmer than centers when nudged with spatula
smell: Sweet, slightly nutty aroma; no raw flour smell
sound: Gentle sizzle; less aggressive than at start
timeHints: Often appears in final 2–5 minutes of baking/roasting window

fallbackSignals: Rotate tray and add 1–3 minutes if edges pale
Watch for rapid shift from pale to golden to prevent burning
rows.safetyNotes: Usually not safety-critical foods; main risk is underdone centers or over-browning.

phrases: roast until golden at the edges

stepTitlePatterns: Roast vegetables until golden at edges

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Look for golden-brown edges and set centers; add short time as needed.

batchSessionIdeas: Batch roast vegetables to golden edges and store for quick meals.
Cookie-baking session: pull sheets when edges are golden and centers pale.

rows.label: Even Browning Top and Bottom
examples: Lasagna with browned cheese on top

visual: Top layer golden and bubbly at edges
Bottom crust or pasta edges slightly browned where contact with pan
texture: Top cheese layer crisp at high points, gooey below
smell: Rich, toasted cheese or breadcrumb aroma
sound: Bubbling sounds around edges when jostled in oven
timeHints: Often requires extra 5–10 minutes beyond simple 'heated through' time

fallbackSignals: Insert knife in center; contents should be hot, not lukewarm
Let rest 10–15 minutes before slicing to set layers
rows.safetyNotes: Casseroles containing eggs, meat, or dairy should be heated thoroughly to at least 165°F in center.

phrases: edges are browned and center is hot

stepTitlePatterns: Bake casserole until bubbly and golden

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Look for bubbling edges and golden browned top; confirm center is hot.

batchSessionIdeas: Batch-assemble casseroles for freezer and bake to even browning when ready.
Use storehouse staples (pasta, canned tomatoes, cheese) for layered casseroles.

rows.label: Fork-Tender Vegetables and Meats

visual: Veg edges slightly softened or rounded
Meat fibers separating easily
texture: Fork slides in with little resistance
Meat pulls apart with fork twist
smell: Deep, blended stew aroma; no sharp raw smells
timeHints: Usually after 1–3 hours low simmer/braise depending on cut and size

fallbackSignals: Test both meat and largest vegetable pieces for tenderness
If tough, continue low simmer in 15–20 minute increments
rows.safetyNotes: Stews with meat should be fully cooked throughout. Once fork-tender, most cuts have passed safe internal temps.

phrases: simmer until the meat falls apart
braise until vegetables are very soft

stepTitlePatterns: Cook pot roast until meat pulls apart

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Check largest pieces with a fork; extend simmer if resistance remains.

batchSessionIdeas: Large stew batch using tougher cuts from butchery; cool and portion for freezer.
Root vegetables cooked fork-tender for mash, soups, or freezer-ready sides.

rows.label: Springy Center (Cakes and Quick Breads)

visual: Top is matte, not shiny
Edges slightly pull from pan
texture: Light press in center springs back without leaving indentation
smell: Sweet, toasty aroma; no raw batter smell
timeHints: Usually in final 5 minutes of listed bake time

fallbackSignals: Toothpick inserted in center comes out with only a few moist crumbs
Avoid overbaking; check early and often
rows.safetyNotes: Undercooked quick breads can be gummy and dense; fully baked crumb improves storage and texture.

phrases: bake until the center springs back
top is golden and center is set

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Press center lightly; it should spring back. Toothpick should have only a few moist crumbs.

batchSessionIdeas: Bake multiple loaves of banana or zucchini bread, cool fully, then freeze.
Use quick-bread sessions to rotate through overripe fruit stock.

rows.label: Nutty / Toasted Aroma

visual: Color deepens slightly from pale to golden or tan
smell: Shift from raw or bland to warm, nutty, toasted aroma
sound: Gentle sizzle or pop; may quiet as moisture evaporates
timeHints: Quick window: often within 30–90 seconds for nuts and butter over medium heat

fallbackSignals: Stir constantly once nutty aroma appears; remove from heat promptly to avoid burning
Err on the side of light toast; darker can tip to bitter
rows.safetyNotes: Primarily a flavor cue. Burning can create bitter compounds and smoke; ventilate when toasting at high heat.

phrases: brown the butter until it smells nutty

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Watch closely; as soon as nutty aroma appears and color deepens, pull from heat.

batchSessionIdeas: Batch-toast nuts or seeds, cool, then store for salads and snacks.
Prep brown butter in batches for sauces and baking; chill or freeze.

rows.label: Caramelized Sweet Aroma

visual: Color shifts from pale to golden to amber or rich brown (onions)
Veg edges browned
texture: Veg tender all the way through
smell: Strong sweet, caramel-like smell; no sharp raw onion smell
sound: Sizzle reduces as moisture cooks off; occasional gentle bubbling
timeHints: Onions can take 30–45+ minutes over low heat
Roasted veg typically 25–45 minutes depending on size

fallbackSignals: Taste a small piece: sweet and mellow, not sharp or raw
If bottom begins to scorch, lower heat and add a splash of water
rows.safetyNotes: Primarily a flavor cue; monitor closely to avoid burning sugars which can smoke.

phrases: roast until vegetables smell sweet and toasty

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Aim for deep golden color and sweet aroma; allow enough time for full caramelization.

batchSessionIdeas: Batch-cook caramelized onions and freeze in small portions for future meals.
Roast and freeze caramelized vegetable medleys for quick sides.

rows.label: Gentle Simmer for X Minutes

visual: Small bubbles occasionally breaking the surface
No violent boiling or splashing
sound: Soft, intermittent bubbling rather than rolling boil
timeHints: Use timer for minimum, but allow extra time for flavor or texture if needed

fallbackSignals: If liquid reduces too quickly, lower heat and partially cover
If not reducing at all, increase heat slightly until gentle bubble pattern appears
rows.safetyNotes: For canning and preservation, always follow specified simmer/boil times and processing instructions in a tested recipe.

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Set a timer and maintain gentle bubbling; adjust heat as needed.

batchSessionIdeas: Schedule broth-making sessions with 60–90 minute gentle simmers.
Plan jam/preserves sessions using garden or storehouse fruit with specified simmer times.

rows.label: Center Slightly Wobbly, Edges Set

visual: Edges look set and matte
Center circle (about 1–2 inches) gently jiggles like firm gelatin when pan is nudged
smell: Warm eggy or creamy aroma; no raw egg smell
timeHints: Usually reached near the end of listed bake time; residual heat finishes the center

fallbackSignals: Insert knife near edge, not center; it should come out mostly clean
Carryover heat will finish setting the center during cooling
rows.safetyNotes: Egg-based dishes should reach safe temps; if uncertain, probe in center to ensure at least ~160°F.

phrases: bake until the center trembles slightly
edges are set but center jiggles

stepTitlePatterns: See if cheesecake center still jiggles

defaultMetadata.cueNotes: Look for set edges and a gentle jiggle in the center; allow cooling to finish the set.

batchSessionIdeas: Bake multiple egg casseroles or quiches and cool until centers set, then freeze portions.
Cheesecake batch for events; cool completely to set before slicing and chilling.
